original_abstract:
Rechargeable zinc (Zn) metal batteries are severely limited by aqueous corrosion, as solvated zinc ions continuously transport active water molecules to the electrode surface. In this work, we introduce a "sacrificial solvation shell" strategy using methanol as an electrolyte additive to displace water from the Zn²⁺ coordination sphere. This approach facilitates the in situ formation of a fluoride-rich, organic-inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. This unique interphase—comprised of an organic-rich exterior and an inorganic-rich (ZnF₂) interior—synergistically provides high mechanical flexibility and rapid ion diffusion paths while physically shielding the anode from water-induced degradation.

The methanol-modified electrolyte enables exceptionally stable and corrosion-free zinc plating/stripping. On copper substrates, we demonstrate over 1,000 cycles with an average Coulombic efficiency of 99.95%. In symmetrical Zn/Zn cells, the system achieves a record-high cumulative plating capacity of 10 Ah/cm² at an aggressive current density of 40 mA/cm². Furthermore, we evaluated the practical potential of this chemistry in VO₂//Zn full cells. Using an ultralow N/P ratio of 2 and a thin 20-μm Zn foil, the battery maintained a high areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm² for over 250 cycles. By achieving commercial-level capacities with thin zinc anodes, this work provides a simplified manufacturing route and a robust strategy for developing long-life, grid-scale aqueous energy storage devices.
humanized_abstract:
Rechargeable zinc (Zn) metal batteries are constrained by the heavy aqueous corrosion. The solvated Zn2+ ions constantly carry wetting water to the cathode surface. Herein, we investigate the effect of adding methanol as a suppressant of water chemistry in Zn batteries, and achieve an electrolyte optimization strategy called a sacrificial solvation shell to effectively replace the water ligands in Zn2+ by adding a proper amount of methanol in electrolyte. A fluoride-rich, organic–inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film is in situ formed on the anode surface in Zn batteries, comprising an organic-rich exterior thin layer and an inorganic-rich (ZnF2) core. Such gradient composition SEI offers remarkable elasticity and fast ion conduction channels and acts as a physical barrier to prevent anode cathode interface from aqueous corrosion.

In situ development of a reliable zinc electrolyte with unprecedented plating/stripping performance and its application in zinc batteries The development of a reliable zinc electrolyte achieved in situ modification of a conventional zinc electrolyte by addition of methanol. A remarkable number of more than 1000 plating/stripping cycles was achieved on a copper substrate with a high Coulombic efficiency of 99.95% and a zinc mass throughput of 200 mAhcm2. Over 10 Ahcm2 (at a current density as high as 40 mAcm2) can be attained in a symmetrical Zn/Zn cell that is 5 times larger than what has previously been reported. Full cells based on a VO2 cathode and Zn anode fabricated from an ultralow N/P ratio of 2 and a simple 20-μm thin zinc foil yield an areal discharge capacity of 4.7 mAhcm2 at the modest volume average loading of 22 mgcm2 with negligible capacity loss during cycling for over 250 cycles. The advancement in the application of zinc batteries herein can significantly reduce cost associated with preparation of Zn battery components, thus paving a path for simpler battery production for large-scale battery applications such as grid scale zinc batteries.
